fr:title.text: The Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Fix a monad {\mathopen {}\left (T, \eta , \mu \right )\mathclose {}} on \mathcal {C}. Its Eilenberg–Moore category arises in two displayed layers. The first layer is the displayed category of algebras \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} of the underlying endofunctor.

The second layer, \mathrm {EMStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}, is displayed over the total category \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}. Over an algebra {\mathopen {}\left (x, \alpha \right )\mathclose {}} the displayed objects are the propositions that \alpha satisfies the monad algebra laws: \eta _{x} \star \alpha = \mathsf {id}_{x} \qquad \mu _{x} \star \alpha = T \alpha \star \alpha .
The Eilenberg–Moore category is the total category of the tower: \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} = \int \mathrm {EMStr}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: Code locations for the guarded recursion implementation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Code locations for the guarded recursion implementation

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Where each note lives in cubical-categorical-logic. Module names are relative to the repository root.

html:ul.html:li: Algebras as a displayed category, Initial algebra — Cubical.Categories.Displayed.Instances.Algebras
Coalgebras as a displayed category, Terminal coalgebra — Cubical.Categories.Displayed.Instances.Coalgebras
The comparison functor, Monadicity and comonadicity — Cubical.Categories.Displayed.Instances.EilenbergMoore.Comparison, …CoEilenbergMoore.Comparison
The adjoint triple, Presheaves are monadic and comonadic over families — Cubical.Categories.Presheaf.Family.Base
Well-founded posets are thin direct categories — Cubical.Categories.Direct.Instances.Poset, …Instances.Nat
Sieves, Proper and maximal sieves — Cubical.Categories.Presheaf.Sieve, Cubical.Categories.Subobject.Base
The strict downset, its maximality, Later on presheaves, Löb induction for presheaves, Later on families, Löb induction on families — Cubical.Categories.Direct.StrictDownset
Locally contractive endofunctors, Local contractivity makes every coalgebra recursive — Cubical.Categories.Direct.LocallyContractive
Hylomorphisms, Recursive coalgebras, The coalgebra-to-algebra profunctor, Unique fixed points — Cubical.Categories.Displayed.Instances.Algebras.Recursive
Selective ordered semirings — Cubical.Algebra.OrderedSemiring and its Selective, MinPlus, Bool submodules

fr:title.text: The comparison functor of an adjunction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The comparison functor of an adjunction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An adjunction F \dashv U with F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} and U : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} induces a monad T = U \circ F on \mathcal {C}. Write \varepsilon : F \circ U \Rightarrow \mathsf {Id} for the counit of the adjunction. Every object d of \mathcal {D} then induces a T-algebra carried by the object U d, witnessed by the map U \varepsilon _{d} : U F U d \to U d.
This assignment extends to a functor into the Eilenberg–Moore category, K : \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}}, the comparison functor of the adjunction.

Dually, an adjunction induces a comonad on the other side and a comparison into the co-Eilenberg–Moore category. When these comparisons are equivalences we say that the adjunction F \dashv U is (co)monadic.

The total category \int \overline {\mathcal {D}} of a displayed category \overline {\mathcal {D}} over \mathcal {C} collects the displayed data into a single category. Its objects are pairs of an object x of \mathcal {C} with an object \overline {x} over it, and its morphisms {\mathopen {}\left (x, \overline {x}\right )\mathclose {}} \to {\mathopen {}\left (y, \overline {y}\right )\mathclose {}} are pairs of a morphism f : x \to y with a displayed morphism \overline {f} over f.

Projecting out the first components is a functor \int \overline {\mathcal {D}} \to \mathcal {C}. Constructions presented displayed — algebras, Eilenberg–Moore categories — get their forgetful functor for free as this projection.

An adjunction F \dashv U with F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} and U : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} induces a monad T = U \circ F on \mathcal {C}, and a comparison functor K : \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} sending each object of \mathcal {D} to the T-algebra it carries.
The functor U is monadic when K is an equivalence: the adjunction exhibits \mathcal {D} as objects of \mathcal {C} equipped with algebraic structure for T, the Eilenberg–Moore category.

Comonadicity is monadicity in the opposite category: a left adjoint L : \mathcal {D} \to \mathcal {C} with right adjoint R induces a comonad W = L \circ R on \mathcal {C}, a comparison \mathcal {D} \to \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}} into the co-Eilenberg–Moore category, and L is comonadic when this comparison is an equivalence.

fr:title.text: Presheaves are monadic and comonadic over families

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Presheaves are monadic and comonadic over families

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The adjoint triple \mathrm {Free} \dashv U \dashv \mathrm {Cofree} induces a monad T = U \circ \mathrm {Free} and a comonad W = U \circ \mathrm {Cofree} on \mathrm {Fam}{\mathopen {}\left (\mathcal {C}\right )\mathclose {}}.

Both comparison functors are equivalences: presheaves are the Eilenberg–Moore algebras of T and the co-Eilenberg–Moore coalgebras of W, \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C} \simeq \mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (T\right )\mathclose {}} \qquad \mathsf {Psh}\,\mathcal {C} \simeq \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}}. So presheaves are both monadic and comonadic over families.

Reading the algebra structure concretely: a T-algebra on a family A is a map \sum _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , y\right )\mathclose {}} \times A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} for each x, subject to the monad algebra laws — that is, exactly a functorial action of restriction.

The comonadic reading is the same structure seen from the element's side: a W-coalgebra is a map A{\mathopen {}\left (x\right )\mathclose {}} \to \prod _{y} \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (y , x\right )\mathclose {}} \to A{\mathopen {}\left (y\right )\mathclose {}}, giving each value its restriction along every morphism into x. Where the monad says restriction acts on values, the comonad says a value already carries all of its restrictions — and the coalgebra laws say it does so coherently.

fr:title.text: The co-Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The co-Eilenberg–Moore category as a displayed category

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A comonad on \mathcal {C} is a monad W on \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}. Everything about its coalgebras is then inherited from the Eilenberg–Moore construction, instantiated at the opposite category — nothing is defined twice.

Algebras of W over \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}} are coalgebras \gamma \in \mathcal {C} {\mathopen {}\left (x , W x\right )\mathclose {}} of the underlying endofunctor, and the monad algebra laws, read in \mathcal {C}^{\mathrm {op}}, are the comonad coalgebra laws — the unit and multiplication of W, viewed in \mathcal {C}, are the counit \varepsilon and comultiplication \delta : \gamma \star \varepsilon _{x} = \mathsf {id}_{x} \qquad \gamma \star \delta _{x} = \gamma \star W \gamma .

The co-Eilenberg–Moore category is the opposite of the total category: \mathrm {coEM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}} = {\mathopen {}\left (\mathrm {EM}{\mathopen {}\left (W\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}^{\mathrm {op}}.

The total category \int \overline {\mathcal {D}} of a displayed category \overline {\mathcal {D}} over \mathcal {C} collects the displayed data into a single category. Its objects are pairs of an object x of \mathcal {C} with an object \overline {x} over it, and its morphisms {\mathopen {}\left (x, \overline {x}\right )\mathclose {}} \to {\mathopen {}\left (y, \overline {y}\right )\mathclose {}} are pairs of a morphism f : x \to y with a displayed morphism \overline {f} over f.

Projecting out the first components is a functor \int \overline {\mathcal {D}} \to \mathcal {C}. Constructions presented displayed — algebras, Eilenberg–Moore categories — get their forgetful functor for free as this projection.
Fix an endofunctor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {C}. The F-algebras form a displayed category \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} over \mathcal {C}.
The total category \mathrm {Alg}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} = \int \mathrm {AlgStr}{\mathopen {}\left (F\right )\mathclose {}} is the category of F-algebras.
